ai_summary.suggested_title: Critical Vulnerability Patches Required Within 3 Days
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your agency's systems are targets — delay patching and you're handing attackers the keys.
ai_summary.action_today: Contact your IT security team: request BOD 26-04 compliance status and patch timeline immediately

articles.title: AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS.
articles.summary: For thirty years, vulnerability management ran on a buffer: the months between when a vulnerability was found and when someone could figure out how to weaponize it. The solution was straightforward enough; triage by severity, schedule the fix, validate, and move on. The buffer was what made that work. Today, that buffer is gone. AI didn't make your team slower. It changed the other side of the

keypoints: AI accelerated exploit development, eliminating vulnerability remediation buffer
CISOs redirecting budgets from patch management to breach and attack simulation

analysis.title: Vulnerability Timeline Collapse
analysis.desc: Traditional 30-year model assumed months between discovery and weaponization. AI now collapses timeline to days or hours.

analysis.title: Budget Reallocation Strategy
analysis.desc: Organizations shifting resources from reactive vulnerability management to proactive breach attack simulation testing.

analysis.title: Defensive Strategy Shift
analysis.desc: Patching speed no longer sufficient. CISOs now prioritize detecting and responding to active exploitation attempts.

ai_summary.suggested_title: Vulnerability Management Dead: CISOs Move Budget to Attack Simulation
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your patch window just disappeared. AI turned your remediation timeline into a liability.
ai_summary.action_today: Schedule meeting with CISO to audit current vulnerability SLA vs actual AI-assisted exploit timeline

articles.title: OceanLotus Hits Vietnam Investors With SPECTRALVIPER in FireAnt Attack
articles.summary: The Vietnam-aligned threat actor known as OceanLotus has been attributed to two distinct campaigns that targeted domestic entities and stock investors with a backdoor known as SPECTRALVIPER. The campaigns involve a prolonged cyber espionage operation aimed at a Vietnamese infrastructure and transport construction corporation between mid-2024 and February 2026, as well as a supply chain attack

keypoints: OceanLotus deploys SPECTRALVIPER backdoor against Vietnamese investors
Multi-year espionage campaign targeting infrastructure and supply chains

analysis.title: Threat Actor Attribution
analysis.desc: Vietnam-aligned OceanLotus group confirmed responsible for coordinated backdoor deployment across multiple victim sectors.

analysis.title: Campaign Timeline
analysis.desc: Prolonged cyber espionage operation spanning mid-2024 through February 2026 against Vietnamese construction and transport entities.

analysis.title: Attack Vector
analysis.desc: Supply chain and direct targeting of stock investors using SPECTRALVIPER malware for persistent network access.

ai_summary.suggested_title: OceanLotus SPECTRALVIPER Backdoor Targets Vietnamese Stock Investors
ai_summary.bottom_line: If you trade Vietnamese stocks or work in regional infrastructure, your systems are actively being hunted right now.
ai_summary.action_today: Check email forwarding rules → Settings → Forwarding and POP/IMAP → verify no unauthorized redirects exist

articles.title: GitHub to Disable npm Install Scripts by Default to Stop Supply Chain Attacks
articles.summary: GitHub has announced what it said are "breaking changes" coming to npm version 12, one of which turns off install scripts by default to combat software supply chain threats. The changes aim to combat attack techniques that abuse the "npm install" command to trigger the execution of malicious code using npm lifecycle hooks. "Npm install" is used to download and install all the necessary

keypoints: npm install scripts disabled by default in version 12
Supply chain attack prevention through lifecycle hook restrictions

analysis.title: Default Script Execution Disabled
analysis.desc: npm v12 turns off install scripts by default, blocking malicious code execution through lifecycle hooks during package installation.

analysis.title: Supply Chain Attack Vector
analysis.desc: Attackers exploit npm install commands to trigger malicious scripts. This change eliminates a primary attack surface in dependency management.

analysis.title: Breaking Change Implementation
analysis.desc: GitHub classifies this as breaking change. Developers may need to explicitly enable scripts for legitimate packages requiring installation hooks.

ai_summary.suggested_title: npm Disables Install Scripts Default — Stops Supply Chain Attacks
ai_summary.bottom_line: Your dependencies won't execute hidden code anymore — unless you explicitly allow it.
ai_summary.action_today: npm → Update to v12 when released → Review package.json lifecycle scripts
